gloss: a book of beasts & their lore
definition: A *bestiary* is a book of beasts: a catalogue of animals, real and fabulous, in which each creature is described chiefly for the moral or spiritual lesson it carries. Where an *herbal* gathers plants and their virtues, the bestiary gathers animals as allegory, treating zoology as a branch of theology rather than natural history.
history: From Latin *bestia*, 'beast', plus *-arium*, 'a place or collection for': a gathering of beasts. The turn is that the medieval bestiary made every animal a sermon — the pelican reviving its young stood for Christ, the ant's industry for virtue. Ron Baxter has argued that houses such as St. Augustine's promoted bestiaries as *sermon exempla*, their parallels between animal customs and human vice running deep into preaching. The genre lends its name to many a Magic card, where a book of beasts is once again a bestiary of the imagination.

def_sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
def_sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

def_sources.label: Sophie Page, Magic in the Cloister
def_sources.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

commentary: The romantic reading made the medieval bestiary proof of a 'symbolic mentality' — a world in which every beast was transparently a moral sign, the pelican simply *meaning* Christ. Recent work complicates the picture from two sides. Ron Baxter ties the genre's rise to a concrete institutional use, arguing that houses such as St. Augustine's, Canterbury promoted bestiaries as *sermon exempla* rather than as free-floating allegory. Sophie Page finds the same books shelved beside marvels and monstrous races — evidence of curiosity about nature's wonders, not only piety. The field now cautions against mistaking the allegory for the whole of medieval seeing.

commentary_sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic (on Ron Baxter)
commentary_sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

commentary_sources.label: Sophie Page, Magic in the Cloister
commentary_sources.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

etymology.note: Books where every animal is a moral lesson — the pelican as Christ, the beaver as chastity. Zoology as sermon.
